criteria: The agent has read Section 2 completely to understand the definitions of Wasserstein distance, one-dimensional projected Wasserstein distance, lifted transportation plan, and their computational properties.

The agent has read Section 3.1 completely to understand the six sliced Wasserstein (SW) distance variants: SW, Max-SW, EBSW (lower bounds) and PW, Min-SWGG, EST (upper bounds), and their empirical estimation procedures.
The agent has read Section 3.2 completely to understand the regression framework of Wasserstein distance onto SW distances, including the unconstrained and constrained linear models.
The agent has read Section 4 completely to understand all five RG model instances (RG-s, RG-e, RG-o, RG-se, RG-seo) and the three main experiment tracks.
The agent has read Appendix B.4 completely to understand the preprocessing protocols for MNIST, ShapeNetV2, MERFISH Cell Niches, and scRNA-seq datasets.
The agent has read Table 1 and Table 3 to check the k-NN classification accuracy results on ShapeNetV2 for all RG variants and baselines.
The agent has read Table 2 to check the R2/MSE/MAE results comparing RG variants against Wormhole across four datasets at training size N=100.
The agent has read Appendix B.5 to understand the five experiments comparing RG-Wormhole vs Wormhole (training time, encoder, decoder, barycenters, interpolation).
The plan includes implementation of the one-dimensional projected Wasserstein distance computation as in Equation 2.
The plan includes implementation of the lifted transportation plan (SWGG) computation as in Equations 3-4.
The plan includes implementation of the standard SW distance via Monte Carlo estimation (Equation 5).
The plan includes implementation of Max-SW distance via optimization (e.g., projected or Riemannian gradient ascent).
The plan includes implementation of EBSW distance via importance sampling with exponential energy function.
The plan includes implementation of PW (projected Wasserstein) distance via Monte Carlo estimation (Equation 6).
The plan includes implementation of Min-SWGG distance via optimization.
The plan includes implementation of EST (expected sliced transport) distance via importance sampling.
The plan includes implementation of the unconstrained linear regression model (Equation 9) with closed-form least-squares solution (Equation 10).
The plan includes implementation of the constrained linear regression model (Equation 11) with least-squares estimation.
The plan includes the five RG model instances: RG-s (SW+PWD), RG-e (EBSW+EST), RG-o (Max-SW+Min-SWGG), RG-se (SW+EBSW+PWD+EST), and RG-seo (all six predictors).
The plan includes the few-shot estimation procedure: sampling M pairs from N to compute regression weights, then predicting on remaining N-M pairs.
The plan includes the point-cloud classification experiment on ShapeNetV2 using k-NN with k in {1, 3, 5, 10, 15} and M0=10 training samples.
The plan includes the low-data regime comparison experiment against Wormhole across MNIST, ShapeNetV2, MERFISH, and scRNA-seq with N in {10, 50, 100, 200}.
The plan includes reimplementing Wormhole in PyTorch (since the original is in JAX/TensorFlow) with the hyperparameters in Table 4.
The plan includes the RG-Wormhole architecture where every Wasserstein call in the Wormhole encoder and decoder is replaced with a calibrated unconstrained RG surrogate.
The plan includes the Gaussian mixture simulation (Appendix B.1) with 3-component mixtures, d=1 to 100, 10 seeds, 200 points per component.
The plan includes the classical methods comparison (Appendix B.6) against Sinkhorn and Linear OT in terms of runtime and R2 accuracy.
The code for computing the one-dimensional projected Wasserstein distance has been implemented as Equation 2 by sorting the projected 1D distributions and computing the quantile function integral.
The code for computing the lifted cost (SWGG) has been implemented as Equations 3-4, constructing the lifted transportation plan using disintegration of measures.
The code for computing the standard SW distance via Monte Carlo estimation has been implemented as Equation 5, using L i.i.d. uniform random directions on S^{d-1}.
The code for computing the Max-SW distance has been implemented by solving the maximization over directions theta via projected gradient ascent or Riemannian gradient ascent.
The code for computing EBSW distance has been implemented using importance sampling with exponential energy weights as in Section 3.1.
The code for computing PW (projected Wasserstein) distance has been implemented via Monte Carlo estimation over uniform random directions, using the lifted cost formula.
The code for computing Min-SWGG distance has been implemented by minimizing the lifted cost over directions theta using optimization (simulated annealing or gradient-based methods).
The code for computing EST (expected sliced transport) distance has been implemented using importance sampling with negative energy weights as in Section 3.1.
The code for the unconstrained linear regression (LSE) of Wasserstein onto SW distances has been implemented with the closed-form solution as Equation 10: omega_LSE = (S^T S)^{-1} S^T W.
The code for the constrained linear regression (CLSE) has been implemented as Equation 11, enforcing the constraint that weights for lower bound and upper bound sum to 1 for each k.
The closed-form constrained LSE for K=1 has been implemented as Equation 12: omega_CLSE = E[(SU-SL)(SU-W)] / E[(SU-SL)^2].
The code for the five RG model instances has been implemented: RG-s (SW+PWD), RG-e (EBSW+EST), RG-o (Max-SW+Min-SWGG), RG-se (SW+EBSW+PWD+EST), RG-seo (all six).
Both constrained and unconstrained versions have been implemented for each RG model instance.
The few-shot regression procedure has been implemented: subsample M pairs from N to estimate regression weights, then use weights to predict Wasserstein for remaining N-M pairs.

The code for loading and preprocessing the ShapeNetV2 dataset has been implemented: centralizing and normalizing to [-1,1]^3, uniformly subsampling 2,048 points per shape, selecting 50 training and 100 test examples per class for the 10-class subset.
The code for k-NN classification on ShapeNetV2 has been implemented using distance matrices computed under each metric with k in {1, 3, 5, 10, 15}.
The code for loading and preprocessing MNIST as 2D point clouds has been implemented by thresholding 28x28 grayscale images at 0.5 and treating active pixel coordinates as the point cloud.

The code for loading and preprocessing ShapeNetV2 for the Wormhole comparison has been implemented: using 15k points per shape, normalizing to [-1,1]^3, splitting into 10k training and 5k test points, sampling 2,048 points per shape.

The code for loading and preprocessing MERFISH Cell Niches has been implemented: scaling gene expression to [-1,1] divided by sqrt(d), using 50 µm radius neighborhoods of 11 nearest neighbors, keeping cells with sufficient neighbors.

The code for loading and preprocessing scRNA-seq data has been implemented: selecting 2,500 highly variable genes, normalizing counts with library-size 10^4 and log(1+x), scaling to [-1,1] divided by sqrt(2500), clustering cells with K-means.

Wormhole has been reimplemented in PyTorch with the Transformer autoencoder architecture using the hyperparameters in Table 4: batch_size=10, Adam optimizer with lr=1e-4, ExponentialLR schedule, 2000 epochs (20,000 steps), num_layers=3, num_heads=4, emb_dim=128, mlp_dim=512, attention_dropout=0.1, coeff_dec=0.1.

The code for RG-Wormhole has been implemented by calibrating a RG surrogate on M0=10 exact Wasserstein pairs and replacing every Wasserstein call in both the encoder loss (pairwise distances) and decoder loss (reconstruction) with the calibrated unconstrained RG surrogate.

The code for the Gaussian mixture simulation (Appendix B.1) has been implemented: 3-component Gaussian mixtures, d=1 to 100 dimensions, 10 random seeds, 200 points per component, computing exact Wasserstein and six sliced metrics.
The code for evaluating R2, MSE, and MAE metrics between predicted and exact Wasserstein distances has been implemented for all experiments.
The code for UMAP visualization of metric-induced geometry has been implemented using pairwise distance matrices computed under each metric on 500 shapes per class from ShapeNetV2 (10 categories).
The code for the classical methods comparison (Sinkhorn and Linear OT) has been implemented for the runtime and accuracy benchmarks in Appendix B.6.

The code for the inter-class and intra-class experiments (Appendix B.7) has been implemented: training on intra-class pairs and testing on inter-class pairs (and vice versa) with 5 experiments per setting and 10 training pairs.
The code for RG-Wormhole encoder quality evaluation has been implemented: computing R2/MSE/MAE between pairwise distances in the learned embedding space and exact Wasserstein.

The code for k-NN classification on ShapeNetV2 (Section 4.1 / Appendix B.2) has been executed successfully for all metrics (WD, SWD, PWD, EBSW, EST, Max-SW, Min-SWGG, and all RG variants) without errors.
The comparison experiment of RG variants vs Wormhole across four datasets (MNIST, ShapeNetV2, MERFISH, scRNA-seq) with N in {10, 50, 100, 200} has been executed successfully without errors.
The RG-Wormhole training-time comparison experiment has been executed successfully, sweeping batch sizes 4-20 and training set sizes N in {10, 50, 100, 200} for both Wormhole and RG-Wormhole.
The Gaussian mixture simulation (Appendix B.1) has been executed successfully for d=1 to 100 with 10 seeds, reporting constrained weights and R2 per dimension.
The UMAP visualization experiment (Appendix B.3) has been executed successfully for ShapeNetV2 across all metrics.

The classical methods comparison (Appendix B.6) has been executed successfully for Sinkhorn (iters=500 and 1000), Linear OT, and all RG variants on ShapeNetV2 pairwise runtime and across four datasets for R2.
The inter-class and intra-class experiments (Appendix B.7) have been executed successfully for both settings on ShapeNetV2 with 5 runs each.
The RG-Wormhole encoder, decoder, barycenter, and interpolation experiments (Appendix B.5) have been executed successfully on ModelNet40.

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 1/3: WD achieves 84.2% at k=5; among single sliced metrics SW and EBSW are strongest at ~72.5% top-1; RG-s and RG-e achieve around 82.5% top-1 with R2≈0.9; RG-se and RG-seo reach up to 83.5% with R2≈0.93.

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 2 (N=100): RG variants consistently outperform Wormhole across all four datasets with Wormhole achieving R2=0.28, 0.65, -3.6, 0.04 on MNIST, ShapeNetV2, MERFISH, scRNA-seq respectively.
The execution outcomes match the results in Table 2: constrained RG variants (RG-s, RG-e, RG-se) achieve R2 of at least 0.84 on MNIST and at least 0.88 on ShapeNetV2 at N=100.

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 2: unconstrained RG variants are generally stronger than constrained variants, with RG-s (unconstr.) achieving R2=0.93 on MNIST and 0.94 on ShapeNetV2.
The execution outcomes match the results in Table 2: on MERFISH and scRNA-seq, RG variants achieve near-perfect R2 (≥0.91 and ≥0.99 respectively) while Wormhole fails badly (R2=-3.6 on MERFISH).

The execution outcomes match Figure 4 (Appendix B.1): R2 is always above 0.8 for all three RG variants across all dimensions; as dimension increases, the constrained weight on the lower bound decreases (upper bound dominates at high dimensions).

The execution outcomes match Figure 14 (Appendix B.5): RG-Wormhole is substantially faster than Wormhole across all batch sizes and training set sizes, with Wormhole's time growing near-exponentially with batch size while RG-Wormhole grows only slightly.

The execution outcomes match Figures 15-16 (Appendix B.5): RG-Wormhole encoder achieves essentially identical R2, MSE, and MAE compared to Wormhole for pairwise distance alignment with exact Wasserstein.
The execution outcomes match Figures 17-18 (Appendix B.5): RG-Wormhole and Wormhole produce very small and nearly identical Wasserstein reconstruction losses.

The execution outcomes match Table 5 (Appendix B.6): RG-s achieves 2.043s for 1,225 pairs vs Linear OT's 15.473s and Wormhole not being applicable for inference; Sinkhorn (1000 iters) is the slowest at 572.441s.

The execution outcomes match Table 6 (Appendix B.6): RG-s achieves R2=0.925, 0.942, 0.964, 0.989 on MNIST, ShapeNetV2, MERFISH, scRNA-seq respectively; Sinkhorn has negative R2 on MNIST and ShapeNetV2.

The execution outcomes match Figures 21-22 (Appendix B.7): RG generalizes well to out-of-sample data with R2 values such as 0.902, 0.846, 0.932, 0.805 in cross-class settings, compared to ~0.95 in-distribution.

The execution outcomes confirm (Figures 6-13, Appendix B.4) that RG variants consistently outperform Wormhole at all training sizes N in {10, 50, 100, 200}, with unconstrained variants slightly stronger and constrained variants converging faster at the smallest sizes.

The execution outcomes confirm (Figure 5, Appendix B.3): RG variants produce UMAP embeddings of ShapeNetV2 that are visually very close to Wasserstein embeddings with well-separated class clusters, while single sliced baselines show more mixing and noise.

The execution outcomes match Figures 23-30 (Appendix B.8): the optimal regression weights vary substantially across datasets, confirming that no fixed set of coefficients universally applies across domains.
The code for computing the exact Wasserstein distance (ground truth) for training and evaluation has been implemented using an LP solver or POT library with O(n^3 log n) time complexity.
The sample-based SW distances matrix S_hat (M x K) and Wasserstein distances vector W_hat (M) have been constructed correctly for computing the least-squares regression weights.
The constrained regression for K>1 has been implemented using quadratic programming to minimize the sum of squared residuals subject to 0 <= omega_k <= 1 for each k.
The computational complexity analysis of the RG method has been verified: training cost O(MKLn(log n+d)) + O(Mn^2(n log n+d)) + O(MK^2+K^3), prediction cost O((N-M)KLn(log n+d)) + O((N-M)K).
The evaluation on 10,000 independently sampled test pairs has been implemented for the low-data regime comparison experiments.
The RG-Wormhole training time experiment has been implemented by training both models under the same optimizer and schedule, measuring wall-clock time while sweeping batch sizes from 4 to 20.
